item.title: Muddy Waters
item.description: Genres: Chicago blues, Electric blues, Delta blues Scene: Chicago (Chess Records) Era: 1948–1958 (Chess peak); active to 1983 RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The voice is unhurried and half-spoken, sure of itself to the point of menace, a man stating his business and daring the room to disagree.

item.title: Blues
item.description: Period: c. 1900–present Geographic origin: African American communities of the U.S.

item.title: Electric Texas blues
item.description: Blues → Electric blues → Electric Texas blues Period: 1942–present Location: Dallas and Houston, later Austin Key influences: Acoustic Texas blues, jazz, jump blues RYM A single-string line that phrases like a horn.

item.title: Electric blues
item.description: Blues → Electric blues Period: 1938–present (peak: late 1940s–late 1960s) Location: Chicago, Memphis, Texas, Los Angeles Key influences: Delta blues, country blues, jump blues, gospel, jazz RYM A guitar string bent through a cheap amplifier in a loud club on Chicago’s South Side, the note hanging in...

item.title: Skiffle
item.description: Folk → Contemporary folk → Skiffle Period: 1954–1958 Location: United Kingdom (London, Liverpool, Glasgow, Manchester) Key influences: American folk, country blues, jug band music, prison work songs, traditional jazz RYM Lonnie Donegan’s “Rock Island Line” opens with spoken narration about a toll-do...

item.title: The color line in pop
item.description: The color line in American popular music is not a metaphor.

item.title: The transatlantic feedback loop
item.description: The pattern that defines the transatlantic movement of popular music — Black American musicians creating a form, white British musicians absorbing and re-exporting it, America responding to the British version — has repeated across so many decades that it functions as a permanent circuit.

item.title: Abbey Road Studios
item.description: Location: 3 Abbey Road, St John’s Wood, London Years of peak activity: 1931–present (as EMI Studios until 1970, Abbey Road Studios thereafter) Key engineers: Norman Smith, Geoff Emerick, Ken Townsend, Alan Parsons EMI opened Abbey Road Studios in 1931 in a converted Georgian townhouse, and for three...
